description: The ten universal vigilances of sufi adab, in English.

vigilances.label: Speech
vigilances.definition_courte: In the sufi way, speech is no ordinary act. It engages the soul. A true word lifts; an empty word weighs down; a wounding word can kill what no gesture could have destroyed.
vigilances.enseignement: The sufi learns to say only what is necessary, true and kind. He prefers silence to useless speech, and the low voice to the cutting one. The tongue is the smallest organ — and the heaviest to carry on the Day of Reckoning.
vigilances.parole_maitre: “Actions are but standing forms; their spirit is the presence of a heart engaged within.” — Ibn ʿAṭāʾ Allāh, Ḥikam no. 9. Applied to speech: without an engaged heart, the word stays empty.
vigilances.verset: “Let whoever believes in God and the Last Day say what is good, or keep silent.” — Hadith reported by al-Bukhārī
vigilances.nom_divin_sens: He is Peace itself — not the absence of noise, but the fullness that lacks nothing.
vigilances.nom_divin_incarnation: To carry this Name is to let your speech lay down peace rather than trouble: to say only what soothes, what is true, what is just.

vigilances.label: Listening
vigilances.definition_courte: Listening, for the sufis, is more than an act of the ears. It is a presence of the heart to what is being said. It begins by welcoming the other without preparing one's reply, without correcting, without interrupting.
vigilances.enseignement: The sufi master listens long before speaking. He knows the other's word sometimes carries the very prescription he needs in order to answer. Not to listen is already to judge. True listening lets the other lay down what he carries, without reducing him to a ready-made image.
vigilances.parole_maitre: “Nothing benefits the heart so much as a retreat in which it enters the domain of meditation.” — Ibn ʿAṭāʾ Allāh, Ḥikam no. 11. Listening is that retreat in which we welcome the other.
vigilances.verset: “Those who listen to the word and follow the best of it — these are the ones whom God has guided.” — Qurʾān 39:18
vigilances.nom_divin_sens: He hears every voice — the heart's whisper as much as the cry. No word is lost before Him.
vigilances.nom_divin_incarnation: To carry this Name is to learn to listen as He listens: without interrupting, without preparing your reply, letting the other lay down what he carries.

vigilances.label: Presence
vigilances.definition_courte: Presence is the heart of the sufi way. To be present is to inhabit the moment as an appointment with God. It is to cease being elsewhere — in regret of the past, in anticipation of the future, in the scattering of the mind.
vigilances.enseignement: Murāqaba — “to know oneself watched” — is the first discipline. It reminds us that nothing we live escapes the divine attention. And so nothing is insignificant. Presence is the art of inhabiting this breath.
vigilances.parole_maitre: “Not a single one of your breaths is without His having foreordained it.” — Ibn ʿAṭāʾ Allāh, Ḥikam no. 20.
vigilances.verset: “We are nearer to him than his jugular vein.” — Qurʾān 50:16
vigilances.nom_divin_sens: He is present to all, attentive witness of every breath, never turning His gaze away.
vigilances.nom_divin_incarnation: To carry this Name is to inhabit the moment knowing oneself watched — and to cease being elsewhere, in the past or the future.

vigilances.label: Patience
vigilances.definition_courte: Patience is more than endurance. For the sufis it is a spiritual station (maqām): to hold before what comes without forcing, without fleeing, without collapsing. Three patiences: before trial, within obedience, and away from transgression.
vigilances.enseignement: Patience is not passivity. It is an inner act: to keep the heart in trust while things take their time. The impatient one wants to prove something; the patient one lets God bring forth what must be, at His pace.
vigilances.parole_maitre: “Do not let the delay of the gift, despite your insistent asking, lead you to despair. He has promised to answer — in a way He chooses for you, not a way you would choose; at the moment He desires, not the moment you desire.” — Ibn ʿAṭāʾ Allāh, Ḥikam no. 5.
vigilances.verset: “And give good tidings to the patient.” — Qurʾān 2:155
vigilances.nom_divin_sens: His patience spans the ages. He is not in a hurry. He lets ripen what must ripen.
vigilances.nom_divin_incarnation: To carry this Name is to endure without hardening: to accept that things take their time, within us and around us, without forcing.

vigilances.label: Repair
vigilances.definition_courte: Repair is the art of returning. Returning to God, to the one we have wounded, to right measure when we have lost it. Repair is not erasing the fall but turning the fall into a path.
vigilances.enseignement: The sufi does not condemn himself. He rises again. True tawba is not content with inner regret — it acts: a gentle word to the one we were harsh with, a silence where there had been rumour, a tiny promise kept after a great one forgotten. To repair is to grow a fruit where there had been a wound.
vigilances.parole_maitre: “Do not despair, even if the fault returns a thousand times; the thousandfold return is still a grace.” — a synthesis of Ibn ʿAṭāʾ Allāh's teaching (cf. Ḥikam no. 47 and no. 150).
vigilances.verset: “Tell My servants that I am the Most Forgiving, the Most Merciful.” — Qurʾān 15:49
vigilances.nom_divin: at-Tawwāb (the One who welcomes the return)
vigilances.nom_divin_sens: He returns endlessly toward whoever returns to Him. The return is inscribed in His very nature.
vigilances.nom_divin_incarnation: To carry this Name is not to freeze in the fault committed — to take up the path again, to ask, to repair what can be repaired.

vigilances.label: Gratitude
vigilances.definition_courte: Gratitude is not merely “thank you.” For the sufis it is a state of the heart: to recognise that all comes from God, and that nothing is owed to us. Gratitude opens; ingratitude closes.
vigilances.enseignement: To recognise what one has received is only a first step. True shukr is lived in use: to use the breath for good, speech for what uplifts, power to relieve. Gratitude is more in deeds than in formulas.
vigilances.parole_maitre: “Let your obedience gladden you — not because it came out of you, but because it came from God to you.” — Ibn ʿAṭāʾ Allāh, Ḥikam no. 56.
vigilances.verset: “If you are grateful, I will give you more; but if you deny My favours, My punishment is severe.” — Qurʾān 14:7
vigilances.nom_divin_sens: He multiplies in return the little one offers Him — a sigh, a word of thanks, a grateful glance.
vigilances.nom_divin_incarnation: To carry this Name is to learn to see the gift in what we took for granted, and to return it by a word, a gesture.

vigilances.label: The Heart
vigilances.definition_courte: The heart, for the sufis, is the central place of the spiritual life — not the seat of emotions, but the organ of the perception of the divine. The heart can be luminous or veiled. The work of the way is to polish the heart so that it reflects again what is.
vigilances.enseignement: Vigilance of the heart is to observe what passes through it without identifying with all that passes. Judgement, resentment, closure, hardness — these are clouds going by. The work is not to deny them but not to feed them. The sound heart (qalb salīm) is the one that does not hold for long what veils it.
vigilances.parole_maitre: “How could a heart be illumined, when its mirror reflects only the figures of the world?” — Ibn ʿAṭāʾ Allāh, Ḥikam no. 12.
vigilances.verset: “Except the one who comes to God with a sound heart.” — Qurʾān 26:89
vigilances.nom_divin_sens: His gentleness acts where none suspects — at the finest, the most discreet, the most delicate.
vigilances.nom_divin_incarnation: To carry this Name is to treat the heart of the other as a fragile thing: in the look, in the tone, in the gesture.

vigilances.label: Service
vigilances.definition_courte: Service, in the Shādhilī way, is central. To serve is not to lower oneself — it is to join the one who has been lowered. It is to carry the other's share when his share is too heavy. It is to love in gestures, not only in words.
vigilances.enseignement: The sufi serves without staging himself. He does the deed that must be done, without announcing, without expecting return. When he serves, it is not to become virtuous; it is because his heart saw a need and answered it. True khidma is silent.
vigilances.parole_maitre: “Companionship is a form, and adab is its spirit. If you join the form and the spirit, you will gain the benefit of their company.” — in the Shādhilī spirit (Laṭāʾif al-Minan).
vigilances.verset: “And do good: God loves those who do good.” — Qurʾān 2:195
vigilances.nom_divin_sens: He is the ultimate source of every good that reaches every creature.
vigilances.nom_divin_incarnation: To carry this Name is to let good pass through you: a service rendered, a presence given, without noise, without expecting return.

vigilances.label: Practice
vigilances.definition_courte: Practice, on the way, is not a performance — it is the watering of the inner garden. Without regular dhikr, without time of silence, the soil dries out. A scattered heart cannot see.
vigilances.enseignement: The sufi knows one cannot be transformed without time of silence. Khalwa is not a withdrawal from the world — it is a withdrawal toward God, even five minutes set down in a noisy day. Dhikr peoples this solitude: a Name repeated, a word carried, a presence cultivated.
vigilances.parole_maitre: “Nothing benefits the heart so much as a retreat in which it enters the domain of meditation.” — Ibn ʿAṭāʾ Allāh, Ḥikam no. 11.
vigilances.verset: “Invoke the Name of your Lord, and devote yourself wholly to Him.” — Qurʾān 73:8
vigilances.nom_divin_sens: He is pure of every imperfection, beyond all that the mind can conceive or imagine.
vigilances.nom_divin_incarnation: To carry this Name is to keep one corner of the day withdrawn from use — a reserved time, a pure act, aimed at Him alone.

vigilances.label: The Body
termes_soufis: ḥaqq al-jasad (the right of the body)
vigilances.definition_courte: The body, for the sufis, is not an enemy to subdue. It is an entrusted mount. It has a right (ḥaqq). To neglect it, exhaust it, despise it — is to betray an amāna, a deposit. To respect it is also to respect the One who shaped it.
vigilances.enseignement: Vigilance of the body is to recognise its fatigue before it dictates decisions, to honour the breath, not to decide from exhaustion. The body carries the voice of the heart when the mind no longer knows. A conscious breath before answering is already a spiritual act.
vigilances.parole_maitre: “Your body has a right over you.” — Hadith reported by al-Bukhārī (the Prophet to ʿAbd Allāh ibn ʿAmr).
vigilances.verset: “Eat and drink, but do not be excessive.” — Qurʾān 7:31
vigilances.nom_divin_sens: He nourishes every creature — from breath to bread, from sleep to health, without missing an instant.
vigilances.nom_divin_incarnation: To carry this Name is to honour the body as a deposit He nourishes: to eat, sleep, breathe as one who receives.
